revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大仙公園]][[zh:大仙公園]]
'''Daisen Park''' (大仙公園) is an urban park (comprehensive park) located in {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai|en|Sakai-ku, Sakai|qid=Q1042008|lt=Sakai-ku|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai}}, {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, Japan.<ref>[http://e-map.city.sakai.lg.jp/sakai/G0303G?mid=1 Sakai City e-Map]</ref> == Overview == Managed by the city of Sakai, the park spans a planned area of approximately 81 hectares. It is located between the {{ill|Daisenryo Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryo Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (mausoleum of Emperor Nintoku) to the northeast and the {{ill|Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun|ja|上石津ミサンザイ古墳|1=Kami-Ishizu Misanzai Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiishizu Misanzai Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ishizu Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} (mausoleum of Emperor Richu) to the southwest, with several small kofun scattered within its grounds. Facilities in the park include the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}}, the Sakai City Central Library, Sakai City tea houses "Shin-an" and "Obai-an," the Sakai City Urban Greenery Center, Peace Tower, and a {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}}. The park was also home to a bicycle museum, which relocated in March 2022 to become the {{ill|Shimano Bicycle Museum|ja|シマノ自転車博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimano Bicycle Museum|1=Shimano Bicycle Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11613133}}. The park is known for its approximately 400 cherry trees, which attract families and visitors for hanami (flower viewing) every spring. Daisen Park has been selected as one of the "[[100 Best Urban Parks in Japan]]," included in the "[[Osaka Green 100 Selections]]<ref>{{Cite web|url= http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/midorikikaku/toshiryokka/hyakusenn.html|title=Osaka Green 100 Selections|publisher=Osaka Prefecture |accessdate=2016-12-23|qq=}}</ref>," and designated as one of the "[[100 Historical Parks of Japan]]" in 2014. == History == The southern half of Daisen Park was originally developed as new farmland in 1629 (Kan'ei 6) by Edo-period officials Takanishi Sekiun and the wealthy merchant Kijiya Shoemon. Initially known as Mozu Shinden, it was later renamed Sekiun Hiraki. In 1925 (Taisho 14), the Osaka Prefectural Agricultural School (a predecessor of {{ill|Osaka Prefecture University|en|Osaka Prefecture University|qid=Q290456|lt=Osaka Prefecture University|1=Osaka Prefecture University}}) moved its campus from Okanocho, Higashinari-ku, Osaka City, to Tsumatsu-mura, Sakai City (later Daisen-cho, west of Daisenryo Kofun). This area later became part of Osaka Women's University. The development of Daisen Park was proposed in 1947 (Showa 22) but delayed until 1967 (Showa 42), after Osaka Prefecture University’s Faculty of Agriculture relocated to the Nakamozu Campus. The park saw significant developments, including the relocation of the Central Library in 1971 (Showa 46), the construction of the Peace Tower and chapel, and the opening of the Sakai City Museum in 1980 (Showa 55) to commemorate Sakai's 90th anniversary as a city. In 1986 (Showa 61), the 37th National Tree-Planting Festival was held in the park, with an imperial visit by Emperor Showa, who performed a commemorative tree planting.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Takeji Hara|title=Showa Emperor's Imperial Train Records|publisher=Shinchosha|year=2016|page=156|isbn=978-4-10-320523-4|qq=}}</ref> == Shin-an and Obai-an == {{Right| [[File:SakaiChashitsu1.jpg|none|thumb|Shin-an]] [[File:SakaiChashitsu2.jpg|none|thumb|Obai-an]] [[File:Daisenpark.jpg|none|thumb|Peace Tower in the center]] [[File:SakaicityMuseum.jpg|none|thumb|Sakai City Museum]] }} The two tea houses, Shin-an and Obai-an, were relocated to commemorate Sakai's 90th anniversary as a city. ; Shin-an : A tea house constructed in 1929 (Showa 4) by renowned sukiya-style architect Ogi Rodō. Originally located in Tokyo's {{ill|Shiba Park|en|Shiba Park|qid=Q3266956|lt=Shiba Park|1=Shiba Park}}, it was donated by Fukusuke Corporation and moved to its current location in 1980. Registered as a [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]] of Japan, it features a standing tea room added during relocation, allowing public access for tea ceremonies. The venue hosted the first match of the Shogi Ryuo Championship in 2007. ; Obai-an : Originally a tea house owned by the Toyoda family in Imai Town (now Kashihara, Nara), it was reconstructed by modern tea connoisseur Matsunaga Yasuemon (nicknamed "the Demon of Electricity") in post-war Odawara. Its name, meaning "yellow plum hut," reflects the timing of its Completion tier 3]] of Japan, it is generally not open to the public. == Key Facilities == * {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * [[Sakai City Central Library]] * Sakai City tea houses "Shin-an" and "Obai-an" * Sakai City Urban Greenery Center * Peace Tower : Built in 1971 to honor victims of the {{ill|Bombing of Osaka|en|Bombing of Osaka|qid=Q2814395|lt=Osaka air raids|1=Bombing of Osaka}} and Sakai’s war dead, this 60-meter tower is the park’s iconic landmark. Though its interior is not open to the public, a staircase allows access to its summit. * {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} : A traditional strolling garden designed by Nakane Kinsaku, incorporating features inspired by continental Asia to reflect Sakai's historical trade links. * Windmill : A replica of windmills once used to pump water for cotton fields in the Izumi area. * Sugifu-sha : A facility displaying specialty products from Higashiyoshino, Nara. == Events == * Sakai City Marathon (April 29) - Starts and ends at Daisen Park. * {{ill|Tour of Japan|en|Tour of Japan|qid=Q1265406|lt=Tour of Japan|1=Tour of Japan}} Sakai Stage (May) - Cycling competition on a park circuit. * Sakai Two-Day March (June) - Nationwide walking event starting and ending at Daisen Park. * Sakai Festival (October) - Includes outdoor tea ceremonies at Shin-an. == Location == Daisen Park, Mozu Yunocho 2, Sakai-ku, Sakai City, Osaka Prefecture. == Access == * **Rail:** 5-minute walk west from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|qid=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line|1=Hanwa Line}} Mozu Station. * **Bus:** 10-minute ride from {{ill|Koya Line|en|Koya Line|qid=Q71986|lt=Nankai Koya Line|1=Koya Line}} Sakai Higashi Station or 20-minute ride from {{ill|Semboku Rapid Railway (company)|en|Semboku Rapid Railway (company)|qid=Q7105599|lt=Senboku Rapid Railway|1=Semboku Rapid Railway (company)}} Fukai Station; get off at Sakai City Museum stop (operated by Nankai Bus). == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Daisen Park|qq=}} * [https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kurashi/koen/shokai/daisenkouen.html Daisen Park Official Site] * [http://www.daisenteien.jp/ Japanese Garden Official Site] {{100 Best Urban Parks in Japan|qq=}} {{Osaka Green 100 Selections|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|大仙公園|version=104637877|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Registered as a [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:1971 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1971]] [[Category:Geography of Sakai Ward]] [[Category:Japanese Gardens in the Kinki Region]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Sakai City]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
